paragraphs.body: Personal Apocalypses

annotations.comment: I find this title a little hard to say, just FYI.

paragraphs.body: I recognize that this draft is a bit longer than ten pages. Feel free to skip the nonfiction thing at the start, I’m not sure about it but I like it so it’s there :)
Questions for consideration:
The goal of this draft was to include as much as possible. What is intriguing? What would you like to see explored more? What doesn’t work?
Are Ant and Hunter consistently characterized?
Doe Hunter have proper motivation?
Hunter’s parents don’t feel right to me. I want them to be a somewhat antagonist force, but I also want it to be clear that they love each other. Thoughts on how to do this?

“In the context of mid-to-late adolescence, a ‘third space’ refers to any recreational area (outside of the home and school) that is (1) free to access and (2) primarily marketed to the teen-aged while being available to the general public. Traditional third spaces, such as parks, arcades and commercial parking lots, have been plentiful as well as wholly acceptable in the eyes of the public for decades, but as the twenty-first century progresses their perception has trended towards the opposite in both respects. The University of Illinois Teen Wellness Study, commissioned by the National Institute of Mental Health and distributed to over eighty thousand thirteen- to seventeen-year-olds over the course of multiple months, found that nearly sixty-three percent of adolescents in the United States do not have regular access to a third space. Moveover, thirty-eight percent of adolescents from this study referenced social media, multiplayer video games, or some otherwise online community as ‘the location that best describes a third space [to them]’ (Gattamorta et. al., 36). The survey also revealed that eighty-five percent of parents of teenagers in the survey, all of which identified as Generation X, do not believe that ‘the lack of a third space [for their children] is a noteworthy problem’ (50). The findings of the University of Illinois, alongside interviews conducted by Dr. Heinrich Danzeisen of the Western Virginia University Sociology Lab (see section 3) have given me cause to believe that the transnational disappearance of third spaces in the United States over the last twenty years has, in part, led to the unprecented rise in online gaming over the same interval.”

annotations.comment: I think you could use this as an epigram (the way Gaiman uses the Sendak quote) but I would end the cutting here.

paragraphs.body: Dr. Halie McCombs, The Empty Green

Like on most nights, Hunter, Ant, and Cece stayed in call later than the other two, preferring each other’s company to the black screen, deafening quiet and uneasy sleep that adolescence perscribed. After all it was summer, and there was nothing to do tomorrow save for it all again. Why waste night when day meant distraction, and expectation? Nonsense. At the moment, Ant was speaking in shrill whispers about a match three hours earlier that neither Hunter nor Cece remembered.

annotations.comment: As onClarify that “other two” means two other players, not one of them staying later the other two named.
Word doesn’t feel right. Enjoying this opening!
Didn’t follow this.

paragraphs.body: “All I’m saying is that they played so strange? They always had some sixth sense for whatever site Ace was on, and the dumbest setups known to man. Bullshit really, but what can you even do about that?” Hunter mumbled in agreement, staring at the streetlight outside as Ant folded his hands behind his head and leaned in his chair. His knee knocked against the bottom of  his desk, causing his camera to shake and unfocus. It refocused, but on a stuffed duck on a nightstand in the background.

annotations.comment: Break paragraph here
Nice moment. Can you help clarify that they are not in the same room?

paragraphs.body: “John Mallard’s stealing your spotlight, baby” Cece giggled.

Just like Ant only acknolwedged people by their online handles (Ace being Jordan), Cece only referred to Ant by weird pet names. Hunter knew that baby was tame by her standards and the world’s but who wanted to be compared to a drooling toddler? It’s the same with the word cute, he thought: reserved for children and small animals. When he got his first girlfriend, he’d call her something sophiscated. Romantic. Not baby.

annotations.comment: This maybe could come up later, this habit of his? I’m not sure I can process all of it right now.

paragraphs.body: Cece went on giggling as Ant waved his hand in front of the webcam to refocus it. This took several attempts, as the stubborn lens locked onto his anime posters and stuffed animals repeatedly before finally settling on his dark eyes and scowl. Sighing, he wiped his glasses on his shirt and pushed the bridge back onto his upturned nose.
“Anyways, you guys have any summer plans so far?”
“No” said Hunter.

“Not until move-in at least” said Cece. The advent of college, a word moreso than a place for Hunter, was the only thing that reminded him of the age difference between Cece and Ant. Two years and four months. Like college, it sat in the background most of the time; they all got along well enough.

annotations.comment: I couldn’t quite follow where Hunter fits in age-wise here.

paragraphs.body: “Has it set in yet?” Ant asked, nursing his knee.
“God no,” she crossed her arms theatrically. “And I’m ready to spend the next two months making sure it doesn’t.”

annotations.comment: It was interesting to me that she wasn’t excited about college.

paragraphs.body: “I’m telling you guys, we lock in and we’re hitting diamond soon. That’s when we’ll plan the group trip,” Ant said with a triumphant smile.

annotations.comment: This might be a place for your narrator to be a little more helpful to us readers, because some of us (not necessarily in our class) do not know what this means.

paragraphs.body: Hunter and Cece laughed, then nodded in agreement— they’d ranked up once before as a group, back when they first met Jordan and Trip. It was a momentous occasion, one when Hunter temporarily forgot it was the middle of the night on a Wednesday and woke both of his parents up. A week of no video games had done nothing to his excitement at finding new friends and teammates, and when he saw Ant in school for the rest of the week they didn’t talk about anything else. Even though he knew it didn’t mean that much in the long run (Ten Tips to Improve Your Aim In Shooters tip eight, to his dismay, noted that there was no percievable difference between the best player in one rank and the worst one in the next) he was undeterable. Plus, he just liked having other people around, though he’d never admit this to himself.

annotations.comment: I thought Jordan was Ant?
This feels like an important bit of information and I’m eager to learn more about these folks so slow down the telling. Let us find out what happened.
annotations.context_right: when he saw Ant in s

annotations.comment: Not deterred.
annotations.context_left: e in the next) he wa

paragraphs.body: As the night wore on, the three of them chatted for a while about the maps they were good and the ones they needed to improve on, the streamers they watched when they weren’t playing themselves, and the skins they’d buy if they became a pro team and got rich. Trip was the only one of them that spent money on the game (excluding Ant’s birthday), so they often spent pre-round trading guns. After a while Ant got up to use the bathroom, leaving Hunter and Cece alone in a usual semi-awkward silence.

annotations.comment: ?Here, too, your narrator can help us understand a bit more.
Explain this

paragraphs.body: Hunter was not sure how Cece was going to pack for college if the rest of her bedroom was as jam-packed as her webcame background. A wooden cabinet backlit with LEDs took up a majority of the space. Each shelf was filled with various trinkets: figurines, loose keychains, books ranging from manga to psycology textbooks, Legos, colorful rocks, all squished together with no obvious organization. On either side of the cabinet suspended on the wall were records, artists that Hunter didn’t recognize but that felt recognizable enough that he was always embarrased to ask about. Under those were neon display signs that matched the LEDs, and under those were piles of colthes. As far as he knew, Cece exclusively tank tops and large sweatshirts, so the khaki always sticking out at the bottom came as a surprise the first time he noticed it. The piles never got bigger or smaller, but they periodically changed colors; were there eighteen-year-olds that didn’t do their own laundary? Hunter’s parents were strict about that. He watched Cece kick a pair of sweatpants into the pile, then fumble with the shoelace that got caught under her chair. Once she looked up at him, he posed a question he’d been thinking about.

annotations.comment: Lotta typos in here, Levi.
Maybe know?
I like this time of sort of wandering over Cece’s possessions as a way of getting to know both of them. I recall being surprised at how other people live and how different it was from my home.

paragraphs.body: “Hey Cece, can I ask you something personal?”
She blew a strand of hair away from her face. “What’s up?”
“When you go to college, are you and Ant gonna break up?”

annotations.comment: I didn’t quite get that they were together. The whole conversation about “baby” seemed to be implying that they weren’t?

paragraphs.body: It took a bit of effort to leave off the word again. Ant was always reclusive when they took breaks, and Hunter wanted to know if he’d be alone going into junior year. He expected her to get mad at him, but she only spun back and forth in her chair for a few moments.
“I’m not sure.” Tenatively, then again. “I’m not sure.”

annotations.comment: This is a rich moment.

paragraphs.body: Even though she didn’t get mad, he still regretted the question. Ant and Cece were both touchy about the on-again-off-again nature of their relationship, but Hunter realized long ago that if he wanted the truth about how they were doing, he’d have to go to Cece. She was the more reasonable, less love-addled one. The older one, he thought. As his mouth opened to form an apology Ant returned and, hearing silence as oppurtunity, launched into a rant about the upcoming patch notes that had just been leaked. He shared the link in the chat, and in opening it Hunter saw that he had a direct message— from Cece, who told him not to tell Ant about what she said. “For me,” it ended. Soon after, the sun peeked in through his blinds and he went to bed.

annotations.comment: I would maybe expand this ending a bit — it feels a little abrupt. I think I missed that it was a significant moment beyond what Cece has to say — that it was important that he asked, too.

paragraphs.body: Chat between Orion91 and S1rAnthony

annotations.comment: I don’t know who these people are.

paragraphs.body: S1rAnthony: Orion.
Orion91: yeah whats up
S1rAnthony: Are you free tomorrow? Between 1500 and 1700.
Orion91: tomorrow today or tomorrow nonday
Orion91: monday
S1rAnthony: Today, but Monday would also be acceptable should your time be predelagated today.
Orion91: also you should hop in vc, trip jordan and i are watching a stream
S1rAnthony: Are you free?
Orion91: yeah
S1rAnthony: Perfect. See you soon, then. Tomorrow today.

annotations.comment: I got confused in here.

paragraphs.body: S1rAnthony: Also, don’t mention this conversation to Ace or 3D. It’s a surprise.
Orion91 reacted [Thumbs Up] to a message from S1rAnthony.

On the way out, Hunter passed by his mother in the kitchen wearing her “One Chef to Rule Them All” apron that he got her for her birthday. Something was sizzling on the stove, and there were eggs on a plate on the counter. One of the yolks ran. The microwave was tilted open towards him, and the clock read two thirty.

“Hi sweetie!” Her voice had that chipper, post-coffee quality to it. There was bacon in the pan that she showed off before grabbing tongs from the side of the drying rack. “I’m making bacon egg and cheese. Do you want one?”
As tempting as it was Ant would probably be at the park by now. He had a thing about punctuality. "No thanks, I'm about to head out."
"Oh? Where to? Certainly not to go see one of your internet friends again.” Bacon sizzled.

annotations.text: Certainly not to go see one of your internet friends again
annotations.comment: Without knowing more about the mom, it’s hard to know how to take this. I would love to see some personality from her, though — moms often seem boring and the same.

paragraphs.body: Internet friends to her were indistinguishable; Cece and random ranked teammates alike were just names on a screen. However, she was talking about a time when Hunter was in middle school and an online friend told him about a gaming convention that was taking place in the next town over. He begged his mom to let him go, clasped his hands together for a full week, did every chore he perpetually avoided until she relented and spent half and hour driving to what was very clearly an abandoned warehouse. His “friend” blocked him, and even though he had the wherewithall to apologize and leave the warehouse right away, she held it over him, he thought, any time he wanted to enjoy himself. Last week Fat Jack’s, the local comic book store, was having a sale on manga and he asked her to go. “And I bet Fat Jack was gonna be waiting for you at that warehouse. You have enough comics,” were her exact words.

annotations.comment: Ah, good context. So maybe she is being a little sarcastic?
Maybe her joke is always about internet friends, though?

paragraphs.body: “Ant and I are gonna hang out.” She didn’t particularly like Ant either, but was she going to tear away his only real friend?
“Alright then. Drink water! And be back for dinner!” she said.

“And watch your step” his dad added from the couch, rolling his eyes at the TV. Two bearded men were walking around a dense forest with guns, blowing some type of whistle. “This town’s going to shit” he added under his breath. Hunter didn’t respond, just walked down the two flights of stairs to the front door. As he closed it, the stove turned off with a tick.

annotations.comment: The dad is a grim surprise in there.

paragraphs.body: Hunter forgot how much he hated being outside in summer. Opening the door hit him with a wave of muck that felt like a physical wall, and as if by instinct his hand reached to his pocket to pull out his phone. No way Ant’s coming out in this heat, he thought, but as he brought the device to eye level to unlock it he got a message.
S1rAnothony: Heat provides cover— on my way. See you in ten.

annotations.text: on my way. See you in ten.
annotations.comment: I’m a little unclear about when Hunter became sure Ant was meeting him. He seems to be sure, then not be sure, then be sure again?

paragraphs.body: He stood on the front steps for a second, considering what they could possibly need cover from, but just shrugged and started walking. After all, Ant said and did a lot of strange things, such as texting like he’s writing an essay and being unnessecarily mysterious. Was it really too important to be DMed? What was even important to Ant? A video game boss theme blasted in his earbuds.

annotations.comment: As much as I am enjoying the story, I did start to lose track of why they’re meeting.

paragraphs.body: The heat only intensified the smell of garbage that permeated the neighborhood, a growing problem as of late. Every single trash can was packed with sacks and scraps and boxes and pieces of things, and every curb was collecting flies. Walking in the middle of the street helped somewhat. In February, the garbage people went on strike in protest of low wages and the city refused to do anything about it. Some families, like Hunter’s, drove their trash to the collection site themselves once a week, but most didn’t care enough to make the hour-long round trip. Or couldn’t, as the dump was only open during regular working hours, and even that was only because the owner of the site worked three four-hour shifts a week himself with his daughter-in-law. The strike was the only thing his family and neighbors and teachers talked about, but nothing would truly allieviate the smell. Hunter thought of Cece, away at college in some far-off place.

annotations.comment: This is a great detail. I wonder if it might come earlier — if the plan to meet outside would bring this up?

paragraphs.body: It took about ten minutes for him to reach the local park, where Ant had requested they meet. Rather, Ant DMed him a set of coordinates that took him to the local park, but there wasn’t any other place in town that wasn’t knee-deep in trash or no loitering. Hunter looked around and spotted Ant staring him down at a bench by the dog water fountain. He made a peace sign with his right hand and Hunter recprocated by making his own, then bending fingers as if making air quotes, before sitting down beside him. That was their secret symbol, a greeting they came up with in fourth grade to determine if they were the friends they’d always been. Secretly, Hunter thought that if he’d truly been cloned, the hand signal wouldn’t do anything because in the movies and TV, clones usually retained your memories and would know what to do. But then, if a clone shares your physical body and lived experience, is it really any different than you? Trip and him had spent an exhaustive two hours discussing this scenario after binge-watching old cartoons on call, and decided that should they be cloned and have to kill one copies of themselves, they’d accept death and live life as the real one. Whatever real means at that point.

annotations.text: That was their secret symbol, a greeting they came up with in fourth grade to determine if they were the friends they’d always been.
annotations.comment: This is adorable.

paragraphs.body: Ant fit the stereotype of chronically online gamer to a tee. He always had some type of crumb stuck to his chin, where there was a stubble that he insisted would grow into a luscious (his word) beard with time. He wore a graphic tee, cargo shorts and his thick circle-rimmed glasses that he loved to push up with two fingers for effect, to seem smart. It wasn’t obnoxious, though, because he was smart: he got great grades, had a girlfriend and was always playing at the strategist in online games. Hunter respected him for it, for the pride he had in his interests and for the lack of embarrassment he showed. Still, he was different in public than behind a computer screen. His voice was always a little too quiet or too loud, and even in a town that stank to high heaven he stood out just a little bit.

annotations.comment: I wonder if Hunter does too?
Nice way of showing that he’s a complex guy.

paragraphs.body: “So, Orion, I must first inform you that Cece and I are once again e-boyfriend and e-girlfriend.”

Not a surprise, considering how they were talking just the other night. Hunter began to voice obligatory congratulations but Ant cut him off. “In addition, we are celebrating our anniversary at the end of the summer. The fifteenth of August. Summer transforms into fall, as does my love ever grow. I was thinking that no gift would be more apt than my presence.” A pleased smile curled on the ends of his lips.

annotations.comment: I’m a little perplexed about whether Cece is at school yet o not.

paragraphs.body: “Like a cardboard cutout of you? That’s silly but she might want a real gift too.”
Ant just kept staring at him.

annotations.text: Ant just kept staring at him.
annotations.comment: Might be helpful to let us know why she’s at school already and also how far a drive it is, etc.

paragraphs.body: “What like you’re gonna drive there? That’s...” he took out his phone and plugged Springfield, Illinois into his map, let it load while Ant still didn’t move, then put the screen up. “...thirteen hours. That’s not going to happen.” What he meant to say was, there’s no way your parents would let that happen. His wouldn’t— they don’t want him to get a liscene, let alone a car. Though if any parents would, it would be his. “And flights are too expensive in addition to not happening,” he added.

“Well I was thinking,” now Ant pulled out his phone, which was opened to their group’s private server. He had made a groupchat, accessible only to two of them, titled Group Trip Secret Plans. Hunter was suddenly aware of his body, as if it came into existence at that moment, and sat up straight. He felt vaguely insulted. At least Ant knew him enough to propose this in person, where he can’t hang up. Hunter knew enough about him to know that this was serious.

annotations.text: Hunter knew enough about him to know that this was serious.
annotations.comment: Did he not see this coming?

paragraphs.body: “You heard what I said right? And you want to just fly Jordan and Trip out, too?”

“Whether or not 3D or Ace want to join isn’t as important. That why it’s our plan. I know it’s a long shot, but you said it yourself— you don’t have a lot to do this summer. Let’s make some money and maybe we can plan something for August.”

For a full minute Hunter listed off reasons why it wouldn’t work, why it’s something they joke about and why Ant was stupid. Ant sat and listened. Nodded a lot. He’d thought about this a lot, clearly, and stayed silent as Hunter got a bit meaner than he’d meant to be. That his eyes didn’t leave Hunter’s was somewhat inspiring. Ant talked about luggage and parents and time and food and finally asked “Why bother? Your dad would probably bring you out there if you asked him. He’d make it a father-son bonding trip.” A twinge of jealousy. He could do that. His parents, but especially his dad, were happy that he had a hobby outside of acing tests.

annotations.comment: Confused about whose dad this was.

paragraphs.body: “It has be with everyone, or at least with you, because that’s what makes it such a great gift. Plus,” he raised his eyebows. “Plussss...” Ant tilted his head forward and down, burning a hole through his eyelids looking up. “You look constipated” Hunter replied, but he understood. Not even Hunter’s dad would let his sixteen-year old son fly three states over to hook up with his online girlfriend. That thought brought to mind the image of the abandoned warehouse. But Ant was committed as always, and Hunter knew that when he got like this, things usually worked out for him. Things usually worked out for him in general. Friend to friend, man to man, he decided to play along. When the trip didn’t work out, they’d have some money to spend.

annotations.text: that’s what makes it such a great gift.
annotations.comment: Hmmm.
Have they ever met before in person? Do they have chats that aren’t with the whole group? Help us know how high the stakes are for this trip.

paragraphs.body: “You’re a perv, and I’m not helping you so you can live out your weird sex fantasies in some Illinois hotel room, and I have no doubt in my mind I will never step foot out of this neighborhood, but clearly you have some plan to convince me because you wouldn’t be squirming in your seat if you didn’t. Go ahead.”

annotations.comment: Definitely intriguing! I liked this a whole lot, Levi. It’s interesting and unique. Most of my queries are about clarifying elements mentioned, which is good — I’m not wondering what the plot is or anything like that. I do think I need a better sense of antagonism here. It’s hard to tell what it is that Hunter wants (and he feels like the main character) but you have given yourself a ton of choices. I also maybe wanted a better initial sense of Cece and Ant as a couple — are they both into it? Why do they keep breaking up? Does Hunter see things more clearly than either of them? Very nice work. Thank you! Keep writing!
